title: What to Build vs What to Buy
concept: Deciding which pieces of the stack to build in-house versus adopt from managed platforms and libraries.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson on designing a new use case, what was the first step before choosing tools or writing code?

options.text: Breaking the user need down into a specific pipeline of LLMs, prompts, and data sources.
Writing a single massive prompt that does everything in one go.
Automatically training a new model from scratch for every minor feature request.

exercises.explain: Designing a new use case requires translating the user need into concrete technical modules (like vector search, prompt templates, and guardrails) before writing any code.

cards.title: Speed vs. Control
cards.body: When building a new AI app, it is tempting to build everything from scratch to have total control. But code you write is code you have to maintain, and in the fast-moving AI world, reinventing the wheel can leave you far behind.

cards.title: Build vs. Buy in LLMs
cards.body: Deciding what to 'build' (write custom code and host internally) versus what to 'buy' (use third-party API services, open-source libraries, or managed platforms) to balance speed, cost, and control.

cards.title: The Pizza Parlor Analogy
cards.body: Building everything yourself is like growing wheat, milling flour, and raising cows just to make a pizza. Buying or using managed services is like ordering the dough and cheese pre-made, so you can focus on baking your unique, delicious recipe.

cards.title: Hybrid Strategy in Action
cards.body: A startup uses OpenAI's hosted API (Buy) to get to market in two weeks, but writes custom system prompts and a unique local routing script (Build) to handle sensitive user data safely.

cards.title: The 'Free' Self-Hosting Trap
cards.body: Many developers think building a custom database or LLM is cheaper because there are no API fees. In reality, the engineering hours, server upkeep, and optimization costs of self-hosting often dwarf API costs.

cards.title: Dividing the Stack
cards.body: A quick comparison of where to spend your energy. Focus on your custom logic, and delegate the heavy infrastructure.

left.text: Proprietary prompt logic, agent workflows, and internal data connectors.

right.text: Base LLM hosting, raw vector databases, and basic server monitoring tools.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following actions represents a 'Buy' (or adopt managed services) decision that speeds up your launch?

options.text: Building a custom database engine from scratch.
Using a managed cloud service to store your vector embeddings.
Training a custom LLM model to replace the database entirely.
Writing your own hardware drivers to speed up local search.

exercises.explain: Using a managed vector database (like Pinecone or Weaviate Cloud) saves weeks of infrastructure setup, allowing you to launch and test your app immediately.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using open-source libraries is always 'free' and carries zero engineering overhead compared to writing your own code.
exercises.explain: Open-source libraries like LangChain or LlamaIndex are free to download, but adopting them still requires engineering time to learn, configure, and maintain. They are tools, not magic shortcuts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase regarding building vs buying strategy:
exercises.sentence: Only build components in-house that provide a unique value to your customers.
exercises.explain: You should only build custom systems in-house if they provide a unique value or competitive advantage that cannot be bought off the shelf.

exercises.prompt: Identify the unrealistic, AI-generated engineering plan:

options.text: We decided to use an API for the base model because training a custom 70B model from scratch would cost us $100k in GPUs before we even find product-market fit.
We will build our own proprietary LLM architecture and custom database engine over the weekend to disrupt the paradigm with synergized cloud native protocols.
Our core differentiator is our proprietary parsing algorithm for medical files, so we wrote that internally and plugged it into a standard cloud LLM.

exercises.explain: Option B uses empty buzzwords and makes a unrealistic promise of building a custom database and model from scratch in 3 days. Real engineers evaluate trade-offs based on specialized needs versus commodity services.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a specialized medical coding assistant. Your company has proprietary guidelines, but limited engineering resources. What is the most strategic build-vs-buy decision?

options.text: Build the vector database from scratch and use a pre-built prompt for your core logic.
Buy/rent the managed LLM hosting and vector database, but build your custom routing and proprietary data integration.
Build a custom transformer model from scratch and rent a standard web template.
Buy the entire system including user data processing and avoid writing any code.

exercises.explain: Proprietary data and custom business logic are your 'secret sauce'—they are unique to your company and cannot be bought off the shelf. Standard pieces like hosting the LLM or running vector databases are heavily optimized by cloud providers, making them ideal to 'buy' or lease.
